title: Indonesia Beats The Count, For Now
gist: Gavekal reports Indonesia avoided the knockout blow of losing MSCI Emerging Market status—preserving access to global investment mandates—but warns the recovery will prove short-lived unless policymakers change course domestically; the oil shock expanded Indonesia's fuel subsidies budget by IDR100 trillion above the IDR210 trillion baseline, stoking fiscal deterioration fears.

themes.theme: Indonesia EM Status and Fiscal Vulnerability

insights.claim: Indonesia avoided losing MSCI Emerging Market status following the oil shock, preventing a further wave of foreign capital outflows; staying in the EM index preserves Indonesian stocks in global investment mandates and provides a confidence boost.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Indonesia's EM index retention is a positive technical signal for Indonesian equities; however, the index threshold risk will persist until domestic policy credibility improves.
insights.locator: Not least Indonesia, which after being hit hard by the oil crisis appears to have avoided the knockout blow

insights.claim: Indonesia's 2026 budget assumed energy subsidies of IDR210 trillion (~$11.8bn, ~1% of GDP) based on $70/bbl crude; after the Iran war started, the government expanded the subsidies budget by IDR100 trillion, sparking fears about Indonesia's deteriorating fiscal position.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Indonesia's fiscal buffer is being eroded by energy subsidies; if oil prices remain elevated, the subsidy expansion could force either painful subsidy cuts (inflationary) or fiscal slippage (credit negative)—a key near-term risk for Indonesian assets.
insights.quote: The government budget for 2026 initially penciled in energy subsidies worth IDR210trn (US$11.8bn), about 1% of GDP, based on a crude oil price of US$70/bbl.
insights.locator: the cost of fuel subsidies will fall dramatically

insights.claim: Gavekal argues the Indonesian recovery could prove short-lived if policymakers do not change tack at home—making domestic policy credibility the key determinant of whether the EM index retention translates into sustained asset price recovery.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Indonesia EM relief rally is a trading opportunity, not an investment thesis, unless evidence of domestic policy reform emerges; watch for subsidy reform announcements and Bank Indonesia communications as leading indicators.
insights.quote: the recovery could prove short-lived if policymakers do not change tack at home
